attributes.stem:
A 50-year-old man is being assessed by his general practitioner as part of a routine review. He has a history of obesity, hypertension, and hypercholesterolaemia. He drinks alcohol on special occasions only and quit smoking 15 years ago. He works as an accountant at a major firm, and is subject to varying amounts of stress dependant upon the time of the year. He admits that he does not get enough regular exercise. Other than complaints of mild fatigue, the patient feels well. He has a family history for significant cardiovascular disease: his father died at 60 from a myocardial infarction and his mother lives with hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia and hyperlipidaemia. He has had persistent mild elevation of his aminotransferases for several years and his practitioner refers him to the liver clinic for further assessment.

Examination is notable for a BMI of 37 kg/m², truncal obesity, and mild hepatomegaly. A fibroscan confirms the presence of significant liver fibrosis. The patient is given advice on lifestyle modifications including weight loss, diet and exercise. The patient wants to know what addition treatment would be most effective in reducing his long term risk.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most effective long-term management in terms of reducing the risk of a major adverse liver event?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH). With his family history and the presence of MASH, cardiovascular problems are a real risk. The focus of management will be on lifestyle measures such as diet regular physical activity and weight loss. Whilst the glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists have come to the fore in the management of diabetes and weight loss, the most effective long-term of weight control in these situations remains some form of bariatric surgery.

According to research (SPLENDOR Trial) published in JAMA in November 2021, patients with (MAS who underwent bariatric surgery, had 88% lower cumulative incidence of major liver events such as cirrhosis, development of liver cancer, requirement for transplantation or liver-associated death; compared to patient who did not have surgery. Other studies exist showing the long-term advantages procedures such as Roux-en-Y gastric bypass over the GLP-1 receptor agonists.

Given that the study clearly demonstrates a greater long term benefit of bariatric surgery over non-surgical care, lifestyle modifications alone is inferior in preventing major liver events. However, it must be stressed that the current primary treatment for MASH is weight loss. Lifestyle modifications should still absolutely be encouraged in patients with MASH to add in the reversal over the condition. However, the question was asking for the most effective long term management of complications.

Liver transplantation is generally considered as a last resort management option for end-stage liver disease, hence would not be considered in this case.

Vitamin E significantly improves liver function in patients with MASH. The potential mechanism of action is that Vitamin E reduces reactive oxygen species and oxidative stress resulting from dysregulated lipid metabolism. However there is limited evidence as to its effectiveness in reducing long-term major liver events.

Patients with MASH are at a higher risk of adverse cardiovascular events. MASH is one of the criteria associated with metabolic syndrome. Hence, whilst statin therapy would almost certainly be used in these patients, it would be to lower their overall cardiovascular risk profile, rather than to treat their MASH solely. Similarly, statin therapy has not been shown to reduce the long term risk of adverse liver outcomes as per the question.

Pioglitazone appears to be an effective treatment for MASH, but there are concerns about its long-term safety. This drug can be associated with weight gain and there may also be an increased risk of cardiac failure
learningPoints: Bariatric surgery is a potentially effective treatment for patients with metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis in terms of preventing major liver events such as cirrhosis, cancer and requirement of transplantation
references: Aminian A, Al-Kurd A. Wilson R, et al. Association of Bariatric Surgery With Major Adverse Liver and Cardiovascular Outcomes in Patients With Biopsy-Proven Nonalcoholic Steatohepatitis. JAMA. 2021;326(20):2031-2042.

Chalasani N, Younossi Z, Lavine JE, et al. The diagnosis and management of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease: practice guidance from the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases. Hepatology. 2018 Jan;67(1):328-57.
Said A, Akhter A. Meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials of pharmacologic agents in non-alcoholic steatohepatitis. Ann Hepatol. 2017 Jul-Aug;16(4):538-47.
Dyson JK, Anstee QM, McPhersonS. Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease: a practical approach to treatment.Frontline Gastroenterology 2014; 5:277–86.

Pouwels S, Sakran N, Graham Y, et al. Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD):
a review of pathophysiology, clinical management and effects of weight loss. BMC Endocrine Disorders 2022; 22:63-72.
Milic S, Mikolasevic I, Krznaric-Zrnic I, et al. Nonalcoholic steatohepatitis: emerging targeted  therapies to optimize treatment options. Drug Design, Development and Therapy 2015:9 4835–4845.

Markovic TP, Proietto J, Dixon JB, et al. The Australian Obesity Management Algorithm: A simple tool to guide the  management of obesity in primary care. Obesity Research & Clinical Practice 16 (2022) 353–363
